message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Data collected from 38 states from August 3 – November 15, 2020 showed more than 250,000 confirmed student and staff cases of SARS-CoV-2 in K-12 schools
                  <jats:sup>1</jats:sup>
                  . Yet, analysis of COVID-19 case data in USA schools has been extremely limited
                  <jats:sup>2,3</jats:sup>
                  . To date, no large-scale or state-wide analyses by school level and grade has been published, opening a wide gap in understanding COVID-19 in American schools. A large-scale assessment of available data and trends could provide a baseline for understanding the virus in the K-12 learning environment and dispel misconceptions about the prevalence of COVID-19 in schools.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Table of Contents Summary</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Using the most comprehensive database of K-12 COVID-19 case data in the country, Florida provides clues for understanding student and staff cases in schools.</jats:p>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>What’s known on this subject</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Florida schools began reopening to in-person instruction in August have reported more than 18,000 student and staff cases of COVID-19 as of November 14, 2020. Incidence of COVID-19 cases in K-12 students and staff is of urgent public health concern.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>What this study adds</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>COVID-19 cases reported in Florida schools were most influenced by community case rates, district mask policies, and percent of students attending face-to-face. Student case rates were highest in high schools (12.5 per 1,000).</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: COVID-19 Trends in Florida K-12 Schools, August 10 – November 14, 2020

reference.unstructured: Jones RD , Glasgow SC , Whaltinez OL . The Covid Monitor. Open data available at https://github.com/TheCovidMonitor/Data
Leana, WS. Most schools should close and stay closed through winter. The Washington Post. 24 November 2020. https://www.washingtonpost.com/opinions/2020/11/24/close-schools-coronavirus-winter/
Jones RD , Glasgow SC , Whaltinez OL . Shut them down: The surprising data behind COVID-19 and schools. US News and World Report. 1 December 2020

reference.article-title: COVID-19 Trends Among School-Aged Children — United States, March 1–September 19, 2020
